claim_under_attack: Zillow should exit iBuying because Zillow Offers' economics are structurally incompatible with the pricing precision the business requires, and the failure of Zillow's claimed pricing edge makes another reform cycle an unattractive board bet.
evidence_or_reason: Side B treats the 6.9% national median Zestimate error as proof of structural incapability, but this conflates a consumer-facing home-value estimate with the buy-side underwriting model. The Zestimate is a public tool; Zillow Offers used a separate, proprietary pricing pipeline with local adjustments, inspection data, and market-specific inputs. The case packet says Zillow 'pivoted the algorithm twice in 2021 to reduce overpayment' — which demonstrates the underwriting model is distinct from the Zestimate and is actively iterable. Side B provides no evidence that a reformed, volume-constrained buy-box operating in proven sub-geographies would still carry 6.9% error. Opendoor's closer-to-breakeven performance in narrower geography (ctx-8) actually supports the thesis that tighter scope improves precision — yet Side B dismisses this as 'not proving Zillow can achieve it' without explaining why Zillow structurally cannot do what a smaller competitor already does. The 'structural mismatch' conclusion rests on treating the worst national aggregate as a permanent ceiling rather than a parameter that shrinks with geographic and volume discipline. This is the central load-bearing claim of the exit recommendation, and it is built on an evidence gap: no sub-geography error-rate data is presented, and the distinction between the Zestimate and the Offers underwriting model is never addressed.
